speaker: interviewer
text: And the interviewer is [? Bea ?] [? Lewkowicz, ?] and I'm interviewing Nora Danzig. We spoke about the-- your recreation in-- in the Jewish children home. Maybe we can go back and I'd like to ask you, how you-- what was it like suddenly to be with Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the home, I think, it was certainly kosher. Because one of the-- there were two girls in this home, and their mother was the cook. And they-- and it was run on kosher lines. How they got kosher meat in-- in Cornwall is beyond my understanding. But I assume that they got kosher meat. It was certainly kept separate. And I think on Friday nights they lit-- candles were lit, I think. I can't-- I can't remember Pesach and so on. But I-- I-- I assume that it was kept. Apart from that, what-- we had no other Jewish things. I don't think we learned Hebrew, because there was no synagogue there. I mean, Cornwall was, to all intents and purposes, the end of the world from London. I'm sure we were the first Jewish people, or children, I should say, that were in this particular area, although it-- it-- it's a holiday resort. And it was a very sweet little village then. Now, it's not so nice anymore. But I'm convinced there were-- that we must been the first Jewish kids there. About Yiddish guide, I-- I can't tell you anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think there were a maximum 15 children, all under the age of 15. I was one of the younger ones, not the youngest. But there were a couple of younger, two or three younger than me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were the other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: The names you mean? You want the names?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, there-- but I get a bit muddled with my-- because I was in, as I said, in two homes. In fact, there was-- there was one called [? Selma, ?] who fondly enough is arriving from America tomorrow. There was [? Marion ?] [? Sonnenfeld. ?] You didn't give me warning of this, or I would have tried to remember more. There was [INAUDIBLE]. I think she's in America now. I mean if I had time, I could probably name you most of them. [? Doris ?] [? Goldbach ?] or [? Goldschmidt, ?] I'm not sure which, [? Tiah. ?] I can't think of anymore at the moment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there are only girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only girls, oh yes. Yes, in those days, you-- you didn't have mixed children's homes, so easily I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there also refugee children?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all refugee children.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were all refugees?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. They were all-- in fact, most of them were Kindertransport children, I think. Two or three of them had family in London. And when the war ended, in fact, they went back to their family. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- I remember one particular girl, called Hela. I can't-- Hela-- what was her name? I don't remember. I only remember the name that she had afterwards. And a couple from London came down to our children's home. They're a very well-known London family. And they picked a child to adopt. What I admire about them is they didn't pick the prettiest or the cleverest. They picked the one that they thought had least chances. And they picked this girl called Hela, and adopted her, and brought her up.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the second child. They already brought up, Hela Dom, became Hela Dom, and she lived in London in Golders Green. And we often used to gather in their-- in her house where she lived. And eventually she went to Israel and was married in Israel. Unfortunately she died now, some years ago. But she has left a family behind her. She had two children and a husband. Apart from that, nothing much sticks in my mind of that particular time of my life. I remember, of course, the matron, who was quite strict with us. She was a bit lenient, as I told you, with me. But the others, I know, often were hit with a stair rod. She would take the stair rod out-- you know what a stair rod is, I think. And she would hit them with this. But she never hit me. I was all right. Because I was so puny and pathetic. So she left me alone. And I used to visit her after the war. She came to London and was in an old-age home in London. And I used to visit her very occasionally.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was-- I don't know her first name. Her name was Miss Jetley, never married, quite elderly. But-- she was, I suppose, in many ways, marvelous. She used to even decorate the-- the-- the home we were in. She used to paint it. So she was quite a fit old lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember, was there any political affiliation of that home?

speaker: interviewee
text: The home was run under the auspices, I believe, of the Highbury Home for Refugee Children. It belonged to-- to that area of London. They had sent the whole home. There must have been one in London, I guess. But I didn't belong to it. I came under the auspices of Hampstead Garden Suburb Care Committee for Refugee Children. So I had-- naturally I had had nothing to do with that one. But I don't know how I got to it. That's where I was, anyway. And then when the war ended, I came back to London in 1945. And I was put in the B'nai B'rith hostel for girls. We had a boys one, as well, nearby.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you said before that you didn't know much about the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, very-- very little really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- was it discussed at all among the children? What do you remember? What were the discussions about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember. I'm sorry. I don't know. And I don't even-- I can't even remember if I had in my mind that I would find my parents. I don't even remember that. I knew my sister, obviously. We-- we corresponded. She was OK. But I knew nothing about my family, the rest of my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your sister at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was left in Ely in Cambridgeshire. And in fact, there were two Jewish teachers in that school. And they took her. When she-- when the war ended, she came back to London as well. And luckily it was near the hostel I was in-- in Cricklewood. And they gave her a home. She lived with these two Jewish teachers, unmarried teachers. So I used to see her, at least, because she lived nearby. And she-- she found that we had a cousin here, from my father's side. We didn't know that. And we found this cousin after the war, who by the time I really got to know, I was married and had two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the war ended, were you happy to leave Cornwall? Do you remember what did it feel like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- you know, I only-- I remember coming back. I must have come back by train, and I can't remember if I came back on my own or if I came with some of the other girls. All I remember is my sister meeting me at the station.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which station?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew were going ask that, because I can't remember. [LAUGHS] I don't know which station. I remember the incident clearly, because again, I came with my one suitcase, and a little hand-- and a handbag that I had given me. And why I remember it so clearly, in those days you trusted everybody. And I put my handbag on top of my suitcase at the bus stop with my 15-- with about 15 shillings in it, which I had saved. And when I turned around to pick it up, it had disappeared. That's why I remember it so clearly. So I lost my-- my worldly possessions, practically. And she must have taken me to this home-- I can't remember-- or hostel. There I was a very unhappy at first, because it was a very big change from the children's home to this girls' hostel. So we were kept-- in-- in Cornwall we were treated like little children. We had to be in bed by half past 7:00. We weren't allowed to go out on our own. And suddenly I came back to London. I was terrified. There was traffic. I wasn't used to traffic to start with. And I was very frightened. And suddenly I was-- seemed to be more-- I had to be responsible for myself. We weren't treated like children there. We had to be home by a certain time in the evenings. And we had to, of course, behave ourselves. We always had to home Friday nights, because of the Sabbath. And I remember, we usually went to synagogue. Again, being a B'nai B'rith hostel, it was kosher. And it was run by a Miss [? Falk. ?] And I was very unhappy there at first. I remember phoning my committee, and saying, if you don't get me out of here, I'm going to run away. I had no intentions of really running away. Because I didn't know where to run to, and I had no money, of course. And I was still at school. So I wasn't earning any money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which grade were you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: By this time I wasn't going to school. I was going to a technical school. It was decided that I was-- as I was good with my hands, I should do dressmaking, which was a big mistake. Because you-- I couldn't earn my living. You were paid so badly that you couldn't live off what you earned. And all-- all the money that I did earn, practically all of it, we had to give for our keep. Once you started to work, you had to pay for your keep. You were allowed to keep a small sum, enough for your fare money, and a tiny bit of pocket money. The hostel closed in late '47, and we all had to move out by this time. I was very-- I was still childish in many ways, but very adult in some ways. And we moved out of the hostel. I and another girl found a room in a house, and-- which we rented, a furnished room. And we lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Willesden Green in London. And it took whatever I earned, it took it all up for just basic, very basic living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still in touch with the sisters after the war? Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes, but my sister, as I think I mentioned, she was very academic. And in those days you couldn't go to university. There was no-- no means of going. So she had passed her high school certificate, which I suppose is the equivalent of A levels these days. And I think she passed with very high grades. She then went to work in a science lab-- in a metallurgy laboratory. And she managed to save a little bit of money, and then eventually she-- unfortunately in a way, managed to get a small grant from one of the Jewish organizations, and she went to Edinburgh, one of the universities in Edinburgh to learn optics. She became an optician.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which year did she go to study to Edinburgh?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been-- in about, I think in about '49. Because I remember I was-- I got married in '49. And she couldn't afford to come. She was in Edinburgh. She couldn't afford to come back for the wedding. She didn't have the fare money. So it must have been around about early '49 is my guess. And she passed her-- her whatever she needed, and became an optician. She passed her exams.

speaker: interviewer
text: In that time after the war, were you in touch with your guardian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was in touch with my guardian, certainly until my marriage. But they-- one after the other, they died. And she gave me a very hard time. I used to try and avoid-- I felt obligated to see her. But I was frightened of her. She gave me some very unhappy times.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one of the first ones that comes to mind-- it sounds so stupid now, I mean-- no girl would react like-- like that now. Perhaps it was my background that I reacted so peculiarly. As I mentioned, I was in a girls' home. And we had to be home by a certain time. And you did, you know, you just didn't rebel. And if you wanted to stay out for the night, you had to have special permission. Now this guardian lived near the hostel I was in, and her sister, the married sister, lived in Baker Street. And she said, we are going to visit Auntie Ester. So I said, all right. And she said, we're going to stay there the night. So I said, I have to get permission for this, which I did. So we went to Baker Street. Bear in mind, I'd only just come back to London, and I didn't know London at all. So we went there, and she decided we wouldn't stay there after all. We'd-- we'd go back to Willesden Green. For some reason, she decided to walk. We would walk. And it's quite a-- quite a long walk from Baker Street to Willesden Green. And all of a sudden, it was getting dark. She left me, and I didn't know where I was. I had no idea, and I had no money. And I-- I became quite hysterical. And she disappeared. I didn't know where she went to. Anyway, to cut-- I don't want to go into the whole details, because it's rather boring, I think. Eventually she turned up, and she insisted we would go back. I think she then took a taxi, and I went back to the hostel, and hid myself. I wouldn't-- in case she came back for me. I was so frightened. And another occasion she insisted I went with her to a lecture in the synagogue. A man had come from Israel to give a lecture. And she started to heckle, which was very embarrassing for a young girl. And people around me said, take her out. I said, I can't take her out. You take her out. You're bigger than I am. And then eventually I ran out, and again, I ran back to the hostel. I was so frightened of her, and hid in case she came. And then when it came to my wedding, she caused me a lot of problems. But it-- I was lucky, in a way, because I thought she was going to appear at the wedding and cause problems. But she was put-- she had to go back to the mental home. So she wasn't at my wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at that time, you found out that she was mentally ill?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I don't think I'd realized that she was mental, although-- well, I didn't have experience of mental illnesses until then. Although apparently she'd been in and out of mental homes for some years. I didn't know about this. Because I was only-- don't forget I was only with her for about a year and a half. But she behaved very strangely. I mean, she did things that-- that you wouldn't do. And when I questioned it, she said, she was making an experiment, conducting an experiment which would help all the children in England. And she was experimenting on me. I mean she didn't hit me or anything. I'm not-- please don't think that-- just mentally disturbed me. And I became very frightened of her. So I used to try and avoid seeing her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Hampstead Garden, the committee know--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think they knew about it, no. I don't think I-- I don't think I reported it, no. In any case, in those days, you know, anybody who would take a child, they gave them a child, because it saved their life, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how they were aware of-- did they respond to an advertisement in the Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how-- I think they must have-- I imagine that in synagogue they would say, is there anyone willing to take a child. Because you know, England was one of the few countries in the-- in the world that took children. I think they took 30,000, or is it-- I'm not quite sure of my facts. They took an enormous amount of young children. America didn't want to know really, only on quota. And I think one or two other countries agreed to take children when it became absolutely desperate, I mean.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your guardian tell you why she took you and your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she ever say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember ever asking. No, I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you came back to London, did you choose to go to the hostel, or would you have rather--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. There was no choice. You were-- there was no choice. You were told. You know, you were put there, and that's all there was to it. I mean I couldn't go back anyway, to the Morrises' which was just as well. Because by then, things had been-- became very difficult for them as well, money wise. They had lost-- I think they had lost their home, because she couldn't carry on her factory. Because it was considered not essential to the war effort. So she'd lost her means of livelihood. And the older sister had lost her means of earning money as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I think they had become very short of money, and they-- they lived in-- I don't know what-- in a small flat, I think. And gradually they all died, every one of them. The four sisters all died. And you said during that period you were in touch with the-- with the committee. With whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry, I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: You said at that period you were-- you phoned the committee.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I phoned my committee, and said, if you don't get me out of this-- this place, I'm gonna run away. But they said-- they said to me, look, give it a month's trial. And if you really are still so unhappy, we'll see what we can do. But in that month, one of the other girls in the hostel sort of took me under her wing, and she happened to be the matron's favorite. So things became better.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was, at that time, Rosemary [? Kroll. ?] She was a very nice girl, and she was very kind to me. And through her, I probably managed to survive that hostel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you speaking to one person on the committee? Was there one person in charge of you? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- you know, I-- I-- I'm sorry. I don't know. I can't remember. There were-- there were two women who were the head of that particular side of the B'nai B'rith. One was called Epstein, and I remember going to her house once. She had a very beautiful home. Apart from that, I had really nothing to do with them. Because I wasn't really-- I didn't come to England under the B'nai B'rith. I came under Hampstead Garden Suburb.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the Hampstead Garden Suburb committee?

speaker: interviewee
text: They seemed to-- I think that must have finished, I imagine. Because I had nothing more to do with them at all after the war. I suppose that ceased. I don't honestly know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, so you were talking about the B'nai B'rith committee?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was then under the-- under the auspices of the B'nai B'rith.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they funded you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: They-- they gave you money when you were in that-- in the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I-- I-- I remember. I got 4 shillings a week, yes, when I was still at school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed there for two years in the-- in the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been a bit over two years. I think the-- the hostel closed in late '47, and I went there in '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many people stayed in the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: How many girls? There were oh, sort of around about, somewhere between 15 and 20, must have been, I think. I'm still in touch with some of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then can you tell me. But you said you moved then to-- out to find your own place?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to. We had to. We had no choice. By that time I was working, and the girl that I shared the room with was also working. So we were able to pay our rent. And we paid-- we each paid a certain amount for food. And I lived there for quite some time. The-- the girl I shared the room with then went to Israel. She decided to go to Israel. But by this time, I was going out with a young man who was also a refugee, and we married in 1949. I was 19 years old, a bit young. And we carried on living in this room. Because we had-- he didn't have any money either. He came after the war to England. And we lived there, and managed to save enough money eventually to put a deposit down on a little house in Kingsbury, which is in London, a suburb of London. So we lived there until about 1953. And I think we moved to this little house in 1954, early 1954.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] We-- a crowd of us from the-- from the hostel went swimming, and he was swimming as well in-- in a swimming pool near us. And he was with the other boys I knew, who were from a boys' hostel. And that's how we met. We met in a swimming pool. But he-- he lived near me. Anyway, I think he was, at that time also in a boys hostel very nearby. And I mean, we used have lots of boys, of course, coming to the hostel. Because there were two or three boys hostels in vicinity. And they used to come, of course, to the girls' hostel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they also B'nai B'rith hostels?

speaker: interviewee
text: One was a B'nai B'rith hostel. I don't know what the other one was, all of course, Jewish. I don't know what the other one-- what auspices the other one-- other one came under.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you find out what happened to your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we found out pretty soon after 1945 that they had perished. I wasn't sure which-- which camp. But I found out since, of course, where they perished. They seemed to survive, I think, till unfortunately till quite late in the war. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I know they were in Theresienstadt, because a soldier was on duty in Theresienstadt, and he saw my parents there. And he was sent home on leave. And when he came back, they had disappeared. And I-- they were sent to Minsk. I believe most of my family died either in Minsk or Auschwitz. I-- only one survivor from both sides of the family-- there is only one survivor from both sides of the family, who lives in New York, and his name is Alfred [? Braunschweiger. ?] And I've seen him several times since.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did you find that out after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I found that out after the war. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My cousin that lived in England, I've mentioned him before, his name was Hugo, Hugo Katz, he changed his name to Kay. He went-- he went after-- soon after the war, back to his home. And he also visited Hosbach to find out if there was any-- if he could get any news. And he brought news back to us, and also two or three little things that neighbors had kept for us. One was a little [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] piece of glass-- I'll call it, a little pendant that my mother handed to this soldier. She was wearing it, and apparently she gave it to this soldier and said, give it to my children, and I have this still today. And some other neighbors had kept some small items of silver, which they gave to my cousin to give to us. And we-- and I haven't got them personally. I gave them to my daughter. And I believe my sister had some, which have disappeared. Because my sister isn't alive anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hope after the war, was there any hope that your parents were still alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think we all hoped that. But that was-- we soon found out that unfortunately they all perished.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then let's go back to your-- the day you married. Can you tell me a bit about your marriage, your wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were very, very hard up. So I didn't have a big white wedding. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mentioned before that I had a very, very distant relative here in London, and she made a tea party for us. I made my own dress. I didn't have bridesmaids, of course. And it was a very simple wedding. It-- it was quite a nice wedding. Unfortunately my marriage didn't last forever. I'm married to a different man now.
